title: Toward Practical Two-Way Covert Communication
authors: Paul N. Fessatidis, Wyatt Wallis, Tae E. Cooper, Mark J. Meisner, Jaim Bucay, Saikat Guha, Shelbi L. Jenkins, Michael S. Bullock, Boulat A. Bash
abstract: We study two-way covert communication schemes, where information is transmitted by passively modulating a reflected signal back to the source. We consider optical systems, described by quantum bosonic channels. While broadband classical and quantum light sources offer high covert throughput in theory, the associated mode-matching and phase-synchronization requirements make them impractical. Therefore, we employ a narrowband laser source to experimentally demonstrate a proof-of-concept two-way covert communication system, where the adversary is assumed to be quantum-capable. Furthermore, we propose a correlator-based receiver that attains the broadband gain offered by a quantum light source without the need for precise mode matching.
summary: This paper tackles the practical implementation of two-way covert quantum communication, aiming to send secret messages without alerting a quantum eavesdropper. The authors overcome the experimental hurdle of mode-matching by proposing a correlator-based receiver using squeezed vacuum states. They provide both theoretical bounds and experimental demonstrations showing how to maximize secure information transfer rates.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of designing practical two-way covert communication schemes that allow information transfer while remaining undetected by a quantum-capable adversary.
main_result: The authors propose and analyze a correlator-based receiver using quantum two-mode squeezed vacuum states, which achieves broadband communication gains without the need for difficult mode-matching, significantly outperforming other analyzed schemes.
method: The work combines theoretical analysis using quantum relative entropy (QRE) bounds for covertness with experimental demonstrations using narrowband lasers and theoretical derivations involving Taylor expansions of quantum operators.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using quantum bosonic channels, involving Alice transmitting to Bob, who then modulates and reflects the signal back to Alice. The analysis considers both broadband (ideal) and narrowband (practical) optical setups.
key_observables: Covert throughput, Quantum Relative Entropy (QRE), Shannon capacity, and the probability of successful range determination ($p_s$).
important_parameters: Channel transmittance ($\eta_i$), thermal-noise mean photon number ($ ar{n}_{Bi}$), and the tolerance to detection ($\delta_{QRE}$).
main_assumptions: The adversary is assumed to be quantum-capable, and the authors often assume lossless channels or specific approximations for the receiver performance.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the communication model, experimental setups (using EDFA/coherent sources), and comparative plots showing covert capacity lower bounds and achievable throughput across different schemes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical limits of covert communication using quantum channels, demonstrating a proof-of-concept with practical narrowband sources, and finally comparing the performance of several advanced receiver schemes, culminating in detailed mathematical derivations of error bounds.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems, as it deals with information security and channel capacity limits in the presence of eavesdropping, requiring deep knowledge of quantum channel characterization and error bounding.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Two-way covert communication model where Willie stands behind Alice, described in Section II-B.

Fig. 2. Experimental setup. The coherent source (Amonics ADFB-1550- 20-B) is split into the signal (green) and reference (orange) arms. Bob and Alice’s EOM’s are Thorlabs LN65S. A Pri-tel FA-18 EDFA injects noise into the channel. We use Yokogawa AQ6370B OSA to collect data at Willie, and a Thorlabs PDB450C balanced detector at Alice.
Fig. 3. Experimental results discussed in Section IV-B.
Fig. 4. Configuration for the proposed correlator-based covert- communication and range-sensing transceiver.
Fig. 5. Simulated throughput of the two-way covert communications schemes described in Section V.
